title: Cat Walking on Hocks: Plantigrade Stance Causes & Triage
meta_desc: Is your cat walking flat-footed? Learn about plantigrade stance, common causes like diabetes, and when to see a vet in our helpful triage guide.
content_html:
TL;DR: A cat walking on its back ankles (plantigrade stance) is often a sign of nerve damage from feline diabetes or an injury and requires a veterinary evaluation within 24 to 48 hours.

What does it mean if my cat is walking on their back ankles?

If you notice your cat walking with their back ankles or hocks touching the ground, this is known medically as a plantigrade stance. Normally, cats are digitigrade, which means they walk on their toes. Seeing your cat transition to a flat-footed gait is often an indication that the nerves or tendons in the hind legs are not functioning correctly.

Is a plantigrade stance in cats a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Medium.
  • While walking on the hocks is typically not a life-threatening emergency requiring an immediate ER visit, it is a significant clinical sign that should not be ignored.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours.
  • The most frequent cause is feline diabetes leading to nerve damage (diabetic neuropathy), but it can also stem from orthopedic injuries or other metabolic imbalances.

How do photos and videos of my cat help the veterinarian?

  • Providing your veterinarian with a photo or a short video of your cat moving in their natural home environment is incredibly beneficial.
  • Cats often experience a surge of adrenaline at the clinic which can mask their symptoms or cause them to walk differently.
  • A clear visual record of the flat-footed gait at home ensures your vet sees the full extent of the issue, leading to a more efficient diagnostic process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

I am unable to provide information on cats, as the provided text excerpts discuss lameness in cattle.

Chapter: Musculoskeletal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1067)

faqs.question: Is walking on hocks painful for my cat?
faqs.answer: While it may not cause the cat to limp or cry out, it represents a loss of strength and nerve function that can make movement difficult and uncomfortable over time.

faqs.question: Can this condition be treated or reversed?
faqs.answer: Yes, many cases—especially those caused by diabetes—can show significant improvement or even complete resolution once the underlying disease is properly managed with insulin and diet.

faqs.question: What are other signs of diabetic neuropathy in cats?
faqs.answer: In addition to the flat-footed gait, you might notice your cat drinking more water, using the litter box more frequently, or having difficulty jumping onto surfaces they used to reach easily.
